unit.name: Multi-head attention and scaled dot-product attention
unit.description: Presents the main transformer innovation over the minimal block: multiple attention heads, efficient matrix implementation, and scaling of attention scores.
unit.summary: The transformer decoder upgrades single-head attention to masked multi-head self-attention so the model can look at different parts of the sequence for different reasons rather than forcing one average pattern of relevance [ts=2544s] [ts=2584s] [ts=2630s]. The lecture rewrites attention in sequence-stacked matrix form—computing XQ, XK, and XV for the whole sequence, forming all pairwise scores with one matrix multiplication, softmax-normalizing them, and multiplying by XV to get all outputs efficiently on GPUs [ts=2716s] [ts=2799s] [ts=2851s]. Multi-head attention then projects into h lower-dimensional subspaces, runs attention independently per head, concatenates the head outputs, and mixes them with a final linear map; this is described as empirically much better than one head [ts=2906s] [ts=2964s] [ts=3255s]. The section closes by motivating scaled dot-product attention: when dimensionality grows, raw dot products get too large and make softmax gradients small, so scores are divided by a dimension-based constant [ts=3399s] [ts=3427s] [ts=3472s].

key_points.text: Multi-head attention lets the model attend to different tokens for different reasons instead of collapsing everything into one pattern.
In matrix form, self-attention computes all pairwise scores with XQ times (XK)^T, then applies softmax and multiplies by XV.
Each head uses its own Q, K, and V matrices and projects from model dimension d to a lower dimension d/h.
Head outputs are concatenated and then mixed by a final linear transformation.
Heads are expected to specialize, but this is not guaranteed and some redundancy remains.
Scaled dot-product attention divides scores by a dimension-based constant so large dot products do not saturate the softmax early in training.

kp_catalog.name: Multi-head self-attention
kp_catalog.description: Instead of one attention operation, transformers run several attention heads in parallel on lower-dimensional projections and then combine their outputs. Multi-head attention runs several independent attention heads in parallel and fuses them, increasing capacity while staying hardware-friendly.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because multi-head self-attention formalizes how instead of one attention operation, transformers run several attention heads in parallel on lower-dimensional projections and then combine their outputs, which later units use as core machinery rather than background context while roles split between gateway and supporting and importance labels split between critical and high.

kp_catalog.name: Key-query-value self-attention equations
kp_catalog.description: Token embeddings are projected into queries, keys, and values; dot-product scores are softmax-normalized and used to compute weighted sums of value vectors.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because key-query-value self-attention equations formalizes how token embeddings are projected into queries, keys, and values; dot-product scores are softmax-normalized and used to compute weighted sums of value vectors, which later units use as core machinery rather than background context.

kp_catalog.name: Scaled dot-product attention
kp_catalog.description: Using a dot product as similarity and scaling it by the square root of the feature dimension stabilizes softmax behavior and gradient flow. Attention scores are divided by a dimension-based constant so large dot products do not saturate the softmax and kill useful gradients.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because scaled dot-product attention clarifies using a dot product as similarity and scaling it by the square root of the feature dimension stabilizes softmax behavior and gradient flow, sharpening later modeling, analysis, or evaluation choices though importance labels split between high and medium.
